checks.a_analysis: The output contains the header, logo description, heading, all five 'WHEREAS' clauses, the resolution statement, the adoption date, and the signature block. All text in the output corresponds to content found in the source document. There are no fabricated paragraphs or sentences.
There are no phone numbers, email addresses, mailing addresses, or URLs present in either the source document or the output. No contact information was fabricated.
The source is a single-page document. All major sections (header, title, preamble, resolution, and signatures) are present in the output JSON. No sections are missing.
The source document contains no tables.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The paragraphs and clauses are fully transcribed. While one minor word was missed (see C6), the overall content of each section is preserved without substantial loss of text.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: In the first 'WHEREAS' clause, the output omits the title 'Rep.' (Representative) between 'US' and 'Norma T. Torres'. The source clearly reads 'US Rep. Norma T. Torres'. This is a minor wording change that slightly alters the identification of the person mentioned.

checks.a_analysis: The output JSON contains each text block from the source document exactly once, preserving the original's content without any duplication of paragraphs, headings, or individual elements.

The JSON uses a single H2 heading for the main resolution title. While standard accessibility often starts with H1, promoting or adjusting heading levels for navigation is an acceptable change. There are no skipped levels or incorrectly nested sub-headings as the document structure is flat.
The source document does not contain any tables, and the JSON output correctly reflects this by not including any table elements.

The reading order in the JSON follows the logical top-to-bottom, left-to-right flow of the source PDF: top header labels, seal image, title, preamble clauses (WHEREAS), resolution statement, adoption date, and finally the signature blocks.
The source document contains series of 'WHEREAS' paragraphs which are structurally paragraphs, not semantic lists. The JSON correctly renders these as paragraphs, preserving the original structure.

checks.check_name: Heading count mismatch between JSON and HTML
checks.a_analysis: The JSON contains 1 heading and the HTML contains 2. The mismatch of 1 heading is less than the 3+ heading threshold defined in the instructions.
There are no links or URLs present in the source document, and the JSON output correctly identifies zero links.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output exhibits significant layout changes and content rearrangement compared to the source PDF. First, the document header ('NORTH CAROLINA' and 'MITCHELL COUNTY') which is justified to the left and right margins at the top of the PDF is collapsed into a single line of text in the HTML and moved below a redundant title header. Second, the signature block at the bottom, which is organized as two distinct columns in the source PDF, has been collapsed into a single-column vertical list in the HTML. This changes the visual organization and flow typical of a formal government resolution.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF features 'NORTH CAROLINA' and 'MITCHELL COUNTY' at the top left/right margins respectively, and two signature columns (Taylor McCurry on the left, Steve Pitman on the right) at the bottom.
checks.b_evidence_output:
The HTML concatenates the header text into '

NORTH CAROLINAMITCHELL COUNTY

' and places it below the

title. The signatures are rendered as a vertical list of paragraphs at the end of the
.
checks.b_evidence_location: Header area and signature area at the bottom of the page.

checks.a_analysis: The source document does not contain any tables.

The source document contains two meaningful handwritten signatures that serve as proof of adoption by the Clerk and the Chairman. While the names are present as text in the HTML, the visual representation of the signatures (images) is missing entirely from the output. In the context of a legal resolution, these signatures are meaningful visual content.
checks.b_evidence_source: Handwritten signatures for Taylor McCurry (above 'Clerk to the Board') and Steve Pitman (above 'Chairman').
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML contains no elements or placeholders corresponding to the handwritten signatures; only the plain text of the names is provided.
checks.b_evidence_location: Signature block at the bottom of the document.

checks.a_analysis: The main title of the document, 'RESOLUTION SUPPORTING THE RECLASSIFICATION OF MITCHELL COUNTY 911 DISPATCHERS', is visually duplicated in the HTML output. It appears once in the <header> block as an <h1> element and again inside the <main> block as an <h2> element. In the source PDF, this title appears only once.
checks.b_evidence_source: The document title 'RESOLUTION SUPPORTING THE RECLASSIFICATION OF MITCHELL COUNTY 911 DISPATCHERS' appears only once, centered below the county seal.
checks.b_evidence_output:
RESOLUTION SUPPORTING THE RECLASSIFICATION OF MITCHELL COUNTY 911 DISPATCHERS

(line 377) and

RESOLUTION SUPPORTING THE RECLASSIFICATION OF MITCHELL COUNTY 911 DISPATCHERS

(line 383).
checks.b_evidence_location: Top of the document and beginning of the main section.

checks.a_analysis: While there are structural and layout issues identified in V1, V3, and V4, the remaining text is readable and the CSS styling for typography and accessibility (skip links, focus states) is of good quality. The issues identified elsewhere cover the primary concerns regarding visual fidelity.
